Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd usually wait until I felt like I absolutely had to do something, then cram in a few hours here and there. With work and other responsibilities, I couldn't really set aside dedicated study blocks. I mostly studied late at night when the house was quiet, maybe twice a week at most. It wasn't organized at all—more like survival mode.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then suddenly realize something was due and scramble to get it done. Regular times weren't really possible for me because my schedule changes week to week. When deadlines piled up, I'd study more intensely, but otherwise I'd drift and let things slide.
Q3: Usually a deadline reminder or an email notification would prompt me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I was missing something important. The gamified tasks they introduced actually got me logging in more often because there was something slightly more interesting to do. But without those nudges, I'd forget the LMS existed for days.
Q4: For a brief period, the gamified elements made me check in more regularly—like it became a habit to see my progress on those tasks. But that wore off after a while when the novelty faded. I never really established a solid routine; it was more like bursts of activity followed by long stretches of nothing. The LMS never felt essential to my daily life.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful to me since I learn best through reading and writing things out. I could download PDFs and go through them at my own pace without needing internet constantly. The discussion posts were occasionally helpful when other students explained concepts differently than the lectures. Videos were okay but I found myself skimming them more than actually watching.
Q6: I mostly ignored the longer video lectures because they took forever and I'd zone out halfway through. Some of the optional supplementary materials I never touched—I just didn't have the energy or interest. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, I probably skipped it. My resources are limited so I had to prioritize what would actually count toward my grade.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly contributed to my grade or helped me understand something I knew would be on the exam. The gamified tasks were briefly engaging because they broke the monotony, but even those got boring after a while. If I couldn't see an immediate payoff, I struggled to care. I needed to see the point clearly.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely skippable to me. Required ones at least had consequences, so I'd force myself through them even when bored. With optional stuff, my low motivation won out every time—why do extra when I'm already struggling to do the minimum? The gamified optional tasks were slightly more appealing but still felt like extra work I didn't need.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded most of the reading materials and studied from those offline since my internet access isn't reliable. I'd print things out at the library and annotate them by hand. I also used my own notes extensively and sometimes looked up explanations on my phone when I could get a connection. A lot of my actual studying happened away from any screen.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reading printed materials or rewriting my notes to understand concepts better, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd work through problems on paper before even attempting online assignments. Sometimes I'd discuss course content with a friend over coffee—real learning happening, zero digital footprint. The LMS only captured a fraction of my effort.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download a PDF or save lecture slides once, then never open them in the LMS again. All my subsequent studying happened from those saved files. It was more practical given my internet situation, and honestly more comfortable than staring at the LMS interface. One download could mean weeks of offline study.
Q12: Around midterm season, everything became harder. I was dealing with work stress, financial pressures, and just general exhaustion. The course content started feeling irrelevant to my life, and my motivation tanked completely. I'd log in, stare at the screen feeling bored and overwhelmed, then close it without doing anything meaningful. That period was rough.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. Competing responsibilities always won—paying bills matters more than discussion posts. Confusion about course material made me avoid it rather than seek help. Fatigue meant I'd log in but just passively scroll without absorbing anything.
Q14: Many times. I'd watch videos while doing other things, not really paying attention. Or I'd have readings open but my mind would wander completely. Sometimes I'd click through materials just to mark them as complete without actually engaging. It was going through the motions—present in body, absent in mind. Boredom made genuine engagement really difficult.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially longer assignments or readings that seemed overwhelming. Even knowing they were important, I'd tell myself I'd do them tomorrow. The lack of immediate consequences made it easy to push things off. My low motivation meant that anything requiring sustained effort got delayed until panic set in near the deadline.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. My LMS activity would spike dramatically in the days before something was due, then flatline afterward. Without deadlines, I'd drift indefinitely. They created artificial urgency that my brain needed to overcome the boredom and actually produce work. It wasn't healthy but it was functional.
Q17: Feedback had minimal effect on me, honestly. By the time I got it, I'd already mentally moved on to the next thing. Good feedback felt nice but didn't motivate me to do more. Critical feedback sometimes made me feel worse and less inclined to engage. I know feedback is supposed to help, but I rarely changed my approach because of it.
Q18: The course structure was flexible, which sounds good but actually hurt me. Too much flexibility meant I could always justify putting things off. The gamified elements helped briefly by adding some structure and immediate rewards. But overall, I needed more external structure to keep me on track—left to my own devices, I drifted badly.
Q19: The self-paced nature made engagement harder for someone like me with low motivation. Having sixteen online courses meant everything blurred together. The gamified tasks made things slightly easier by breaking content into smaller, more manageable pieces. But the sheer volume of content across all my courses was overwhelming and made me want to disengage entirely.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—logging in, clicking around, maybe watching videos passively, but never finding the motivation to actually complete and submit work. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. You can spend hours in the LMS feeling bored and overwhelmed without producing anything. The system tracks presence, not purpose.
Q21: That actually describes me somewhat. My exam score was decent despite low attendance and sporadic LMS activity because I studied effectively offline. Reading and writing things out on my own worked better than engaging with online content. Some people just learn differently, and the LMS doesn't capture independent study. Performance and logged activity aren't the same thing.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you log in, what you click, how long pages stay open. But they miss everything meaningful: whether you're actually thinking, learning offline, struggling emotionally, or just going through the motions. They can't measure boredom, confusion, or the quality of engagement. It's quantity data pretending to be quality data.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to the material. It's not just logging in or completing tasks—it's wanting to understand, being curious, feeling like the work matters. For me, engagement was rare this semester. I completed things, but genuine engagement? That requires motivation I mostly didn't have.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my constant struggle with boredom and low motivation. It wouldn't capture the offline studying that actually helped me learn, or the financial and work stress that drained my energy. It wouldn't show that I was going through the motions while feeling disconnected. My decent grades hide how disengaged I actually felt throughout the course.
